dopamine-detox.title: Dopamine Detox
dopamine-detox.subtitle: Break digital addiction in 30 days
dopamine-detox.description: Step out of phones, social media, and fast dopamine. Reset your brain.

silent-morning.title: Silent Morning
silent-morning.subtitle: 21 days to a monk mode morning ritual
silent-morning.description: Wake early. Don't open your phone. Live the first hour in silence.

mind-discipline.title: Mind Discipline
mind-discipline.subtitle: 28 days to deep focus mastery
mind-discipline.description: Single focus. Distraction-free work. Build your deep work muscle.

body-discipline.title: Body Discipline
body-discipline.subtitle: 30 days to reclaim your body
body-discipline.description: Movement, food, cold shower. Hardness for your body.

money-discipline.title: Money Discipline
money-discipline.subtitle: 21 days to financial hardness
money-discipline.description: Stop wasteful spending. Build a budget. Form saving habits.

1.title: The Science of Addiction
1.teaching:
Dopamine isn't a reward chemical — it's an anticipation chemical. Your brain releases dopamine while imagining a future reward, not when the reward arrives.

While scrolling your phone, every video, every notification is a small dopamine hit. Your brain learns: 'Phone = reward exists.' Result: constantly checking your phone, reaching for it when bored, real life feeling 'flat.'

Good news: this learning is reversible. Your brain settles into a new baseline in 7–30 days. You're starting now.
1.action: Every time you open your phone today, wait 10 seconds before looking at the screen. Just wait. Then do what you wanted.
1.reflectionPrompt: Did you notice how many times you opened your phone 'without thinking' today?

2.title: Kill Notifications
2.teaching:
Every notification creates a micro-explosion in your attention. Research: it takes 23 minutes to fully refocus after one notification.

If you get 50 notifications a day, you're never fully focused. Your brain is in constant 'is something incoming?' mode.

Notifications don't serve you — they serve apps. Their growth metrics. You pay the cost.
2.action: Turn OFF every notification on your phone. Keep only calls and direct messages. Do this now. If you postpone, you won't.
2.reflectionPrompt: Which notification was hardest to disable? Why?

3.title: Social Media Fast
3.teaching:
Social media is one of the fastest dopamine sources. Infinite scroll, unpredictable content, social comparison — you've plugged your brain into an optimized exploitation machine.

Today you start a test: 24 hours, no social media. Instagram, TikTok, Twitter, YouTube Shorts — all of it.

The first few hours will be hard. You'll auto-unlock your phone and reach for the app. This is 'phantom reaching.' Normal. It passes.
3.action: Remove all social media apps from your home screen. Put them in a folder named 'Enemies.' Don't open any of them for 24 hours.
3.reflectionPrompt: When did the urge 'just one quick check' come up? What were you feeling at that moment?

4.title: Cut Junk Content
4.teaching:
Junk content = empty calories for your brain. Random YouTube videos, Reddit scrolling, news refreshing, podcasts only as background.

What you don't notice: this content isn't just making you dumber — it's making you an accessory. Your brain gets used to consuming others' thoughts and forgets how to produce its own.

Today we install a filter: every piece of content you consume must serve a purpose.
4.action: Don't consume any content today. Only produce. Write, draw, walk and think, have a real conversation. No information consumption today.
4.reflectionPrompt: How many times today did you feel 'information hunger'? What was that hunger really for?

5.title: Allow Boredom
5.teaching:
Modern life is programmed to fill every empty moment. Phone in the elevator. Phone in the bathroom. Headphones while walking. Video while eating.

Result: your brain never enters idle mode. Creativity is born in idle mode. Solutions come in idle mode. Self-knowledge happens in idle mode.

Boredom isn't a sickness — it's a signal. Your brain saying: 'I need empty time.' Listen to it.
5.action: Do nothing for 30 minutes today. No phone, no music, no book. Just sit or walk. Observe your thoughts. If you get bored, great — keep going.
5.reflectionPrompt: What thought emerged in your mind by the end of 30 minutes? Were you aware of it before?

6.title: Phone Out of Bedroom
6.teaching:
Sleeping with the phone next to your pillow shreds sleep quality. Blue light suppresses melatonin, night notifications fragment sleep stages, the morning's first act becomes scrolling.

What's your last screen before sleep? First screen on waking? These determine your mental health. If both are the phone, your life happens in the gap between screens.

Today we create physical distance. Charging the phone in another room is simple but radical.
6.action: Tonight, charge your phone outside your bedroom — in another room. When you wake up, spend 30 minutes phone-free first.
6.reflectionPrompt: Did your time-to-sleep change without the phone in the room?

7.title: Don't Touch the First Hour
7.teaching:
When you wake, your brain is in 'cortisol awakening response' mode. The first 30–60 minutes are when perception, motivation, and day-planning capacity are highest.

If you reach for your phone in that hour, you're plugging your brain into others' agenda: notifications, emails, social feeds — you've lost control of your day.

The first hour is yours. Use it, or someone else gets your whole day.
7.action: Tomorrow, don't touch your phone for the first 60 minutes after waking. Drink water, wash your face, go outside, eat breakfast — phone-free.
7.reflectionPrompt: How did the phoneless first hour feel? Empty, or calm?

8.title: Grayscale Mode
8.teaching:
Colors are extra dopamine fuel for the brain. Red notification dots, green checkmarks, glowing visual content — they create subconscious pull.

iOS and Android both have a 'grayscale' mode. When you turn it on, the phone suddenly looks 'boring.' That's a powerful psychological circuit-breaker.

This isn't a hack. It's science. The brain has been hijacked, we're returning it to its natural state.
8.action: Switch your phone to grayscale. iOS: Settings > Accessibility > Display & Text Size > Color Filters > Grayscale. Use it for 24 hours.
8.reflectionPrompt: Without color, which app did you lose interest in fastest?

9.title: Cut Porn
9.teaching:
Porn is the most extreme form of modern dopamine exploitation. Unlimited variety, instant gratification, unreal stimulation — it desensitizes the brain to real relationships.

NoFap research is clear: after 30 days off, energy, motivation, natural interaction with the opposite sex, and overall self-esteem rise.

This isn't shame. It's basic discipline for an optimal brain.
9.action: Starting today: 30 days, no porn. Install a site-blocker (Cold Turkey, BlockSite). Decide today.
9.reflectionPrompt: What objection came to mind when you made this decision?

10.title: Day 10 — Mid-point Reflection
10.teaching:
You've finished a third of the 30-day detox. What have you noticed?

The first week is hard. The second week the brain pushes back and tricks you ('what's one slip?'). The third week the new baseline settles.

Change your body language. Look in the mirror. Are you standing taller? Eyes clearer? These small signs are the first markers of change.
10.action: Make today a reflection day. Sit for 15 minutes and write: (1) What changed in the first 10 days? (2) Which moment was hardest? (3) Which habit didn't slip?
10.reflectionPrompt: What's the BIGGEST change you noticed in the first 10 days?

11.title: Change the Environment
11.teaching:
Willpower is a finite battery. In behavior change, environment matters more than willpower. If chocolate is in the fridge, you'll eat it. If your phone is on the bed, you'll check it.

Physical distance = willpower savings. You don't think about what you don't see. You don't want what you don't think about.

Today we clean the environment of enemy triggers.
11.action: Look around your home. Pick 5 dopamine triggers and physically remove them: home-screen apps, junk food, alcohol, gaming console — whatever they are.
11.reflectionPrompt: Which trigger did you think would be hardest to keep 'out of sight'? Why?

12.title: Embrace Boredom Again
12.teaching:
In the first week you met boredom. Now it's time to love it. Boredom is the empty space your brain needs to form new connections.

Dopamine deficiency is the emergence of mental space. In that space, you ask real questions: 'What do I want in life?' 'Am I happy in this job?'

Before, those questions were drowned out by phone scrolling. Now you face them.
12.action: Spend 60 minutes bored on purpose today. Walk alone, sit in a room, stare at the ceiling. No phone, no music, no book.
12.reflectionPrompt: What big question surfaced while you were bored?

13.title: Single-Task Discipline
13.teaching:
Multitasking is a myth. The brain can't truly do two tasks in parallel — it switches fast. Each switch drops performance by 40% and increases errors.

Real deep work happens with a single task. Phone off, single tab open, music off (if it distracts).

This is training. Day 1, 25 minutes of single-focus is hard. A week later, 90 minutes feels natural.
13.action: Do one 90-minute single-task block today. Pick one task. Phone in another room. Only one tab open. 90 minutes only that.
13.reflectionPrompt: How much more did you finish in 90 minutes? In 'multitask' mode, how long would the same task have taken?

14.title: Cut Artificial Stimulation
14.teaching:
Caffeine, sugar, fast music, aggressive video games — all are artificial dopamine spikes. They create stimulation above the natural baseline.

In the short term, feeling 'energetic' is normal. Long-term, the brain pulls baseline up — meaning normal life feels increasingly 'flat.'

If you reduce artificial tools during detox, natural motivation comes back. You may seem less energetic, but more sustainable.
14.action: Cut caffeine in half today (one coffee). Skip sugar entirely. Work in silence. Spend the day on natural energy only.
14.reflectionPrompt: Without artificial stimulation, what was my energy level? How did it feel?

15.title: Return to Real Connection
15.teaching:
The detox isn't about isolation. It's about replacing fake connections (social media, message threads) with real ones.

A 10-minute deep conversation is more nourishing than 10 hours of DMs. Digital communication is like food with zero calories — you eat but don't get full.

Return to real connection today.
15.action: Call (not message) someone you care about today. Talk for at least 15 minutes. The topic doesn't matter — the connection does.
15.reflectionPrompt: What did real voice-to-voice feel like compared to texting?

16.title: Past Halfway — Acknowledgment
16.teaching:
Today is day 16. You crossed the half. Two inner voices are now clearer:

(1) Old you: Wants fast dopamine back. Looks for excuses. 'What's one break?'

(2) New you: Calm, decided, less reactive. Loves the empty space.

14 days left. The hardest part is behind you.
16.action: Buy yourself a gift today — but not ordinary. A book, quality meal, nature walk. Low dopamine, high meaning.
16.reflectionPrompt: Between the two yous (old and new), which was stronger today?

17.title: Breath Discipline
17.teaching:
When stressed, the search for dopamine increases. Stress = urge to escape. The brain reaches for the phone.

Breathing breaks this loop. The 4-7-8 technique: inhale 4 sec, hold 7, exhale 8. 4 rounds = 1 minute. The brain shifts to parasympathetic mode.

This is the alternative that replaces the phone. In moments of stress: breath, not phone.
17.action: Do 4-7-8 breathing 3 times today. After waking, at lunch break, before sleep.
17.reflectionPrompt: What did you notice after the breath? Did the urge to grab the phone change?

18.title: Digital Minimalism
18.teaching:
Cal Newport's concept: delete every digital tool you don't need. If it doesn't add real value to your life = it's exploiting you.

10 apps are enough for most people. Messaging, navigation, banking, camera, calendar, music, podcasts, email, weather, shopping.

The rest are enemies. Social media, games, unnecessary shopping apps — they pull a minute at a time.
18.action: Review EVERY app on your phone. Delete every one you haven't opened in the last 30 days. Delete every one you hesitate on. You can re-install if needed — you won't need to.
18.reflectionPrompt: How many apps did you delete? Which was the hardest to delete?

19.title: Slow Down
19.teaching:
The fast-dopamine habit creates the expectation that 'everything must happen now.' Waiting in line is unbearable. Cooking takes too long. Reading is slow.

The detox is rewriting these expectations. Slow things bring pleasure again.

Today: slow down. Refuse the rush. Take an hour for what would have been 30 minutes.
19.action: Eat one meal slowly today — no phone, no screen. Chew each bite for 20 seconds. Enjoy the food.
19.reflectionPrompt: Which flavors came back when you ate slowly? Did you feel fuller?

20.title: Day 20 — New Baseline
20.teaching:
Your brain may have recalibrated dopamine receptors. The 'flat' feeling is actually the new normal. Did you notice life doesn't suffer without stimulation?

Most people are surprised at this point: 'Less is actually better.' That's a big teacher.

10 days left. Now you'll learn how to harden this new baseline.
20.action: Celebrate day 20 — but not with a dopamine bomb. Do a quiet activity with a loved one. Walk, eat together, nature.
20.reflectionPrompt: What's the biggest difference between the 'you' 20 days ago and now?

21.title: Recognize Your Triggers
21.teaching:
Every dopamine craving has a trigger. Boredom, sadness, loneliness, fatigue, sense of failure — these create the 'escape' urge. The escape usually goes to the phone.

Knowing your trigger is power. Once you know it, you break the automatic response. 'I'm bored' becomes 'I'm reaching for the phone because I'm bored, but my real need is to sit with the boredom.'
21.action: Today, every time a dopamine urge hits, write one sentence: 'Right now I feel X, I want the phone.' Look at the list at night.
21.reflectionPrompt: Which trigger repeated most? What does that emotion really want?

22.title: Replacement Strategy
22.teaching:
Just deleting a bad habit isn't enough. What will you replace it with?

Breath replaces the phone. Walking replaces scrolling. Cold shower replaces breakfast scrolling. Books replace bingeing.

What prevents post-detox relapse: new behaviors taking root. What will you put in place today?
22.action: Write a replacement list for the next 5 hours: when the phone urge comes, what will you do? (E.g., 4-7-8 breath, 10 squats, drink water, go outside.)
22.reflectionPrompt: Which replacement worked? Which felt fake?

23.title: Negative Visualization
23.teaching:
The Stoics practiced 'premeditatio malorum' — imagine the bad in advance. 'What would it feel like to lose this detox?'

Imagine 1 hour back in your old life: scrolling, junk food, random video. Then compare it to where you are now in this detox.

Loss aversion is twice as powerful as gain. Use it as a weapon.
23.action: Close your eyes for 5 minutes. Imagine a 24-hour day where you weren't on this detox — old phone habits, old fatigue. Then return to now.
23.reflectionPrompt: What did imagining the old day feel like?

24.title: Make the Phone a Tool
24.teaching:
The phone is a tool. You use it; it doesn't use you. After the detox, this should be your relationship.

When it's a tool: you open it intentionally (I'll do X), do it, close it. You don't open it for entertainment. You don't open it from boredom.

After the detox you can re-install some things. But with the new rule: intentional use.
24.action: Today, before opening the phone, say out loud 'I'm going to do X.' When done, close it. No entertainment.
24.reflectionPrompt: How did intentional use compare to 'just looking' use?

25.title: Final Sprint Begins
25.teaching:
Last 5 days. The most critical phase of the sprint. The brain needs to finish this last week to settle into the new baseline. Otherwise you have a 'half-detox' and easy relapse.

You may feel difficulty rise this week. The thought 'It's done, I made it' often leads to early surrender.

The finish line is day 30. 25, 26, 27, 28, 29, 30. Finish.
25.action: Write your last-5-days commitment today: Which habits will you keep permanently? Which apps will you NEVER reinstall?
25.reflectionPrompt: Which app, even though the detox is over, will you not reinstall? Why?

26.title: Social Immunity
26.teaching:
When the detox ends, the environment will pull you back to old habits. Friends are on the phone. Family is in WhatsApp groups. Social media topics are everywhere.

For immunity, two things: (1) Don't forget why you started. (2) Inform your circle — 'I've cut down on phone use, if I reply late it's normal.'

People will respect it. Those who don't belong to your old life — not your new one.
26.action: Today, message 3 important people: 'I'm in the last week of a detox, my phone is mostly away. Don't worry if I reply late.'
26.reflectionPrompt: How did people respond? As you expected?

27.title: Permanent Boundaries
27.teaching:
The detox is temporary, the boundaries are permanent. This weekend the detox ends — but what rules will stay?

Some should be permanent: notifications off. No phone in the bedroom. Don't touch in the first hour. Social media once a day for 15 minutes.

These aren't 'rules for your life' — they're 'limits for your brain.' Science says: maintain at least a year and the new baseline becomes permanent.
27.action: Write 5 permanent boundaries. Very specific: 'After 9pm phone is in another room charging,' 'I will never use TikTok,' etc.
27.reflectionPrompt: Which of the 5 will be hardest to maintain?

28.title: Practice Meaning
28.teaching:
Dopamine = momentary pleasure. Meaning = lasting fulfillment. Exploitative dopamine is forgotten in an hour, meaning is carried for years.

Dopamine is reduced after detox; now fill it with meaning. Meaning: purposeful work, real bonds, skill acquisition, service.

Meaningful things don't give 'fast pleasure' but at the end of the day make you say 'it was worth it.'
28.action: Do something meaningful today: Help someone (a neighbor's groceries, call an elderly relative). No gift, expectation, photo. Just the act.
28.reflectionPrompt: How did the meaningful act feel? Not dopamine — what was it?

29.title: Tomorrow Is the Last Day
29.teaching:
Day 29. Tomorrow is 30. Could you have sustained 30 days of discipline before this journey?

Most people can't. You did. This isn't just dopamine detox — it's proof of discipline.

Tomorrow is the end day, not the start. You enter your new life with this hardened mind.
29.action: Write today the last 29 days — short: 'For 29 days I did dopamine detox. These changed: ...' Save it. One day you'll come back to read it.
29.reflectionPrompt: Could the you of 29 days ago have written this sentence?

30.title: Complete — New You
30.teaching:
30 days. It's done. But really, just begun.

Your brain has recalibrated. Your dopamine baseline has dropped — meaning small things give pleasure again. You tolerate boredom. The phone no longer controls you — you control it.

This hardness is yours now. No one can take it.
30.action: Today, celebrate the whole sprint. Meaningfully — not with a big meal but with a real meeting with loved ones. Phone in bag, off.
30.reflectionPrompt: After 30 days, what's the MOST IMPORTANT change for you? How will you protect it?
